questions.q: Which market-share figures does the guide give for Linux?
options: About 3% of personal computers, more than 82% of smartphones
About 30% of personal computers, 50% of smartphones
Under 1% of personal computers, 99% of smartphones
About 10% of personal computers, 25% of smartphones
questions.why: Android uses a Linux kernel; almost 70% of the world's websites are hosted on a Linux or UNIX server.

questions.q: Since 2018, how many of the top 500 supercomputers run Linux?
options: 100% - all of them
About half
The top 10 only
None - supercomputers use custom operating systems
questions.why: Linux has equipped 100% of the top 500 supercomputers since 2018.

questions.q: In the guide's architectural design, what is the kernel?
options: The heart of the system: it manages hardware resources, and other software must go through it to access the hardware
The utility that interprets user commands
The set of user programs like browsers and word processors
The graphical desktop environment
questions.why: Kernel = first software component, the heart; the shell interprets commands; applications are user programs.

questions.q: Which are named as the main shells?
options: Bourne shell, C shell, Korn shell, and Bourne-Again shell (bash)
bash, zsh, fish, and PowerShell
sh, dash, ash, and csh
Korn shell, Z shell, tcsh, and cmd
questions.why: The shell is the utility that interprets user commands and ensures their execution.

questions.q: Linux is a time-sharing (multi-task) system. Which of these is listed as a consequence?
options: All of: simultaneous execution of several programs, CPU time distributed by the scheduler, reduced impact of a failed application, and reduced performance when too many programs run
Only one user may be logged in at a time
Programs must cooperate to yield the processor
The kernel restarts after each program finishes
questions.why: Time-sharing = the OS divides processing time between programs, switching transparently.

questions.q: Where does Linux's multi-user ability historically come from?
options: MULTICS, whose purpose was letting several users work from terminals on one (expensive) computer
Windows NT domains
The Apache web server
The C language's standard library
questions.why: Each user gets an account with memory space and access rights to files and software.

questions.q: What makes Linux 'open' in the guide's terms?
options: It is based on recognized standards - POSIX, TCP/IP, NFS, Samba - so it can share data and services with other systems
Its source code has no licence at all
Anyone may push commits to the kernel without review
It only runs open-source applications
questions.why: Multi-platform too: PCs, servers, smartphones/tablets, embedded systems, routers/switches, even TVs and refrigerators.
